coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emissive carbon dots for determination of silver ions
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mengying
Liu, Yuxiang
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a one-step solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and dl-malic acid was used to obtain blue/yellow-emitting carbon dots (B/Y-CDs). The B/Y-CDs have two independent fluorescence emission peaks centered at 453 nm and 584 nm at a single excitation wavelength. In the presence of silver ions (Ag+), the fluorescence at 453 nm was quenched and the fluorescence at 584 nm was enhanced by dynamic quenching and redox reaction mechanisms, respectively. The “Double-response-Reverse” ratiometric fluorescent probes constructed on the basis of the B/Y-CDs showed a good linear relationship with Ag+ in the range of 1–90 μM. In addition, the feasibility of this detection strategy for the detection of Ag+ in real water samples was verified, and the probe showed satisfactory recoveries, which could be applied to the quantitative analysis of Ag+ in real environmental water samples. This work provided a new strategy for the identification and detection of Ag+, enriching the ways of detecting Ag+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission
carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent probes
Silver ions
Solvothermal method
Real water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples
Possible mechanism for detection of Ag+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HRTEM image), (b) size distribution histogram, (c) FTIR spectra, (d) XRD pattern of B/Y-CDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum of B/Y-CDs. The corresponding high-resolution spectra of (b) C 1s (c) N 1s (d) O 1s. (e) Probable model of B/Y-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence excitation spectrum of B/Y-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of B/Y-CDs at Em = 265–305 nm.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs with different concentrations of Ag+. (b) The linear relationship between F584/F453 and the Ag + concentration. Ag + concentration is 0–90 μM.

(a) Effect of different metal ions on the relative fluorescence intensity of B/Y-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs with Fe3+, Fe3++F−. The concentrations of Ag+ and other metal ions were 5 μM and 50 μM, respectively.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of Ag+ and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of B/Y-CDs. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of B/Y-CDs in the absence and presence of Ag+. (c) XPS full spectrum of B/Y-CDs in the presence of Ag+, the corresponding high-resolution spectrum of (d) Ag 3d and (e) C 1s. (f) The high-resolution spectrum of C1s of B/Y-CDs.
Mechanism of Ag + detection by B/Y-CDs.
Schematic representation of the preparation of B/Y-CDs and their application for Ag+ detection.
Spiked recoveries and RSDs (%, n = 3) for the determination of Ag+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emissive carbon dots for determination of silver ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a one-step solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and dl-malic acid was used to obtain blue/yellow-emitting carbon dots (B/Y-CDs). The B/Y-CDs have two independent fluorescence emission peaks centered at 453 nm and 584 nm at a single excitation wavelength. In the presence of silver ions (Ag+), the fluorescence at 453 nm was quenched and the fluorescence at 584 nm was enhanced by dynamic quenching and redox reaction mechanisms, respectively. The “Double-response-Reverse” ratiometric fluorescent probes constructed on the basis of the B/Y-CDs showed a good linear relationship with Ag+ in the range of 1–90 μM. In addition, the feasibility of this detection strategy for the detection of Ag+ in real water samples was verified, and the probe showed satisfactory recoveries, which could be applied to the quantitative analysis of Ag+ in real environmental water samples. This work provided a new strategy for the identification and detection of Ag+, enriching the ways of detecting Ag+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver, discovered and used thousands of years ago, is a precious metal with a long history [1]. Silver ions (Ag+) have unique antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties and are used in water storage and purification [2], as well as in the treatment of wound infections and closure [3]. In modern times, silver has found an even wider range of uses, such as in electronics, photoreceptors, catalysts, and medicine [4–6]. The vast array of silver-based materials naturally enters the ecosystem, posing a significant threat to the environment and human well-being. Ag+ that enter water have toxic effects on prokaryotes, fish, plants and other organisms [7,8]. Excessive intake of Ag+ may result in altered neurotransmitter levels, increased heart size, weakened immune system, or even death [9]. As a result, the development of sensitive and reliable analytical techniques for detecting Ag+ is of paramount importance for human health and exploring the biological toxicity of silver.

Thus far, methods for detecting Ag+ consist of atomic absorption spectrometry [10], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], electrochemical analysis [12], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering [13]. While these methods may provide precise analytical outcomes for Ag+ analysis, they are limited by the utility of costly instruments, intricate sample preparation, and interference susceptibility. Fluorescent probes have garnered considerable attention in recent years due to their notable benefits such as low cost, ease of use, and excellent selectivity [14–17]. Within this category, ratiometric fluorescent probes stand out for their ability to overcome the drawbacks of single-emissive fluorescent probes, which are prone to background noise, matrix effects, and variations in probe concentration [18]. With their built-in self-calibration feature, ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibit greater sensitivity and detection accuracy. In recent years, several sensing types of ratiometric fluorescent probes have been developed [19,20]. Therein, compared with the ratiometric fluorescent probe in which one signal is quenched or enhanced with the other unchanged, or both signals are simultaneously quenched or enhanced, the “Double-response-Reverse” ratiometric fluorescent probe produce two opposing signal changes in the presence of the target analyte, effectively avoids a false-positive signal response [21]. It is capable of significantly improving the sensitivity of the signal response to the target. Currently, metal nanoparticles [22], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [23], and organic dyes [24] have been successfully used to construct many excellent ratiometric fluorescent probes, but they inevitably faced some challenges, such as high cost of raw materials, difficult to prepare, high toxicity, and poor photostability [25]. Thus, it is essential to develop ratiometric fluorescent probes with low cost, low toxicity, and resistance to photobleaching.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a size less than 10 nm, are gaining attention in the Ag+ detection field for their low toxicity, superior optical stability, favorable biocompatibility, and simple surface functionalization and modification [26–29]. For example, Jiang et al. [30] designed hemicellulose/deep eutectic solvent-based CDs with bright blue fluorescence and well selective and sensitive detection for Ag+. Li et al. [31] successfully synthesized blue sulfur-doped CDs using bisulfide hydrazones as precursors and combined them with magnetic ion imprinting solid-phase extraction to achieve the enrichment and detection of Ag+. You et al. [32] mixed blue CDs with red CdTe QDs to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for Ag+ detection. Fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection based on CDs are predominantly single-emissive probes, fewer ratiometric fluorescent probes are presented, and most of the ratiometric fluorescent probes are prepared by physically mixing two fluorescent materials with different emission wavelengths, which is a redundant step [33,34]. Wu et al. [35] designed an ultrasensitive ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N, Si-CQDs/CdTe QDs for the detection of Ag+ by a simple hybrid approach. The design and synthesis of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs with simple steps, sensitive and rapid detection remains a matter of interest.

In this study, blue/yellow-emitting CDs (B/Y-CDs), which are dual-emissive CDs, were designed and successfully prepared by a convenient one-step solvothermal method using dl-malic acid and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as precursors. At the same excitation wavelength, the photoluminescence peaks of B/Y-CDs appeared at 453 nm and 584 nm. Upon addition of Ag+, the blue fluorescence peak of B/Y-CDs was quenched while the yellow fluorescence peak was enhanced. Based on this, a “Double-response-Reverse” ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed for the detection of Ag+. In addition, the practicality of the method was verified by detecting Ag+ in real environmental water samples.

dl-malic acid, o-phenylenediamine (o-PD), NaCl, NaF, MgCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, AgNO3, CoCl2·6H2O, MnCl2, FeCl3, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaH2PO4·2H2O, and ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). CaCl2, CuCl2 and ZnCl2 were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The above reagents were analytical reagent grade and require no further purification for use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained with a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained with an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were obtained using an F-97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang, China) and an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh, United Kingdom), respectively. UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured with a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

B/Y-CDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method using dl-malic acid and o-PD as precursors. Briefly, 268.2 mg of dl-malic acid and 648.8 mg of o-PD were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol, and after thorough mixing, the solution was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and the reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 10 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was then further purified in a dialysis bag (MW = 1000 Da) for 12 h. Finally, the purified B/Y-CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

Typically, 0.1 mL of B/Y-CDs solution was taken and mixed with 1.8 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 8.5), and then 0.1 mL of Ag+ solution of different concentrations was added. After thorough mixing, the reaction was carried out at room temperature for 4 min, and the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 290 nm. To ensure the accuracy of the experimental results, each group of samples was measured three times in parallel.

Selectivity study experiments were performed to investigate the selectivity of B/Y-CDs for Ag+. Ag+ was replaced by other metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+ and Fe3+) to verify the selectivity of B/Y-CDs for Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the effectiveness of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe in actual water samples, environmental water samples from the industrial area of Xiaotangshan Town, Changping District, Beijing District were selected as test samples, and the recoveries of Ag+ in real samples were analyzed. Firstly, the water samples were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Then, the clear liquid portion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected and added with different concentrations of Ag+ standard solutions. The analytical method was performed as described above and the spiked recoveries were calculated by linear regression equation.

B/Y-CDs, which were CDs that exhibit dual-emission with blue and yellow fluorescence, were synthesized via the solvothermal method utilizing dl-malic acid and o-PD as precursors. Upon the addition of Ag+, the blue fluorescence of B/Y-CDs was quenched due to the dynamic quenching effect, and the yellow fluorescence of B/Y-CDs was enhanced due to the redox mechanism (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of B/Y-CDs was affected by the ratio of raw materials, reaction time, and other factors. The proportions of reactants were adjusted by keeping the amount of o-PD constant while varying the amount of dl-malic acid. The experimental results demonstrated (Fig. S1) that the fluorescence response of B/Y-CDs was most intense at a dl-malic acid dosage of 2 mmol. The fluorescence response of B/Y-CDs remained relatively unchanged when adjusting reaction time under the optimum raw material ratios. Seen from Fig. S2, 10 h was determined to be the optimal reaction time.

The morphology, lattice structure, and size of B/Y-CDs were analyzed by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the B/Y-CDs were uniform in size and had a monodisperse spherical structure with good solution dispersion. The diameters of the B/Y-CDs were distributed mainly between 2.0 and 5.0 nm, with an average particle size of 3.60 nm (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image of the B/Y-CDs (inset in Fig. 1a) indicated that the lattice spacing of the B/Y-CDs was 0.31 nm, which corresponded to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [36]. Fig. 1c showed the FTIR spectra of B/Y-CDs. The absorption peak at 3221 cm−1 was the O–H/N–H stretching vibration, the absorption peak at 1733 cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretching vibration, and the peak at 1667 cm−1 indicated the presence of CN. The absorption peaks at 1592-1437 cm−1 resulted from the backbone vibration of the aromatic ring. The peaks located at 1274 cm−1 and 1175 cm−1 corresponded to the C–N and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively, while the peak at 747 cm−1 corresponded to the C–H bending vibration in the aromatic ring. Fig. 2 d displayed the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of B/Y-CDs. A diffraction peak was observed at 2θ of 22°, which corresponded to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [36], in agreement with the HRTEM analysis.

The chemical composition and surface structure of B/Y-CDs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2a was the full XPS spectrum of B/Y-CDs, which showed that B/Y-CDs were composed of three elements of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, and the three main peaks at 285.1 eV, 399.7 eV, and 532.2 eV originated from C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, with the corresponding atomic percentages of 71.45 %, 16.71 %, and 11.84 %. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2b) were decomposed into three fitted peaks at 284.8 eV (CC/C–C), 286.4 eV (C–N/C–O), and 288.7 eV (CO), and the high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) were fitted into three peaks at 398.5 eV, 399.8 eV, and 400.1 eV, which were attributed to pyridinic N, graphitic N, and aminic N, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 2d) contained two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV, which were associated with CO and C–O, respectively [37]. These XPS results were consistent with the FTIR analysis. In summary, there were –NH2, –OH, and –COOH on the surface of B/Y-CDs. Moreover, the characterization results were consistent with those reported in the publications [38], and it was speculated that the 2,3-diaminophenazine structure was on the surface of B/Y-CDs (Fig. 2e).

The optical properties of B/Y-CDs were examined. As indicated in Fig. 3 a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of B/Y-CDs exhibited two discernible absorption peaks at 235 nm and 273 nm resulting from the π-π* electron transitions of CC and the n-π* electron transitions of CO, respectively [37]. In addition, the maximum excitation wavelengths of the fluorescence emission peaks of B/Y-CDs at 453 nm and 584 nm were 277 nm and 333 nm, respectively, and the intersection of the two excitation spectra was located near 290 nm. As shown in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 270 nm to 310 nm, the emission peak in the blue-emitting region was increased gradually, while the emission peak in the yellow-emitting region was increased in the beginning and then decreased, and the emission peak in the yellow-emitting region reached the maximum at the excitation wavelength of 275 nm. Both peaks of B/Y-CDs remained consistent regardless of the excitation wavelength, indicating excitation independence. It also demonstrated that the peak at 584 nm was the yellow fluorescence emission peak of B/Y-CDs rather than the frequency doubling peak.

The optical stability of B/Y-CDs was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S3, under 30 min of light exposure, the fluorescence value of B/Y-CDs at 453 nm was slightly decreased, and the fluorescence value at 584 nm was basically unchanged, indicating good photostability. In addition, the influence of different pH and salt concentration on the fluorescence properties of B/Y-CDs were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the ratios of fluorescence intensities of B/Y-CDs at 453 nm and 584 nm was hardly changed in the range of pH 5.5 to 10, indicating that they had good pH stability and could be used in acidic or alkaline systems. Then, the impact of salt concentration on B/Y-CDs was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs in PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 8.5) at different concentrations of NaCl. Fig. S5 illustrated that the fluorescence intensity ratios remained stable when the B/Y-CDs were in various NaCl concentrations from 0 to 500 μM, which indicated that the prepared B/Y-CDs had excellent stability despite the environment of higher saline concentrations. In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of B/Y-CDs was 2.43 %.

To enhance the detection performance of the probe, the effects of excitation wavelength, detection pH, and reaction time on the experimental results were investigated to obtain the optimal detection conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the two fluorescence excitation spectra obtained with 453 nm and 584 nm as the emission wavelengths, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelength was 290 nm, the fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs exhibited two peaks with higher luminous intensity and less overlap at 453 nm and 584 nm. Consequently, 290 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength for the subsequent experiments. Then, the impact of different pH levels on the detection of Ag+ using this probe was studied. The protonation and deprotonation effects of groups on the surface of B/Y-CDs, such as –COOH and –NH2, present in non-neutral solutions affected the interaction between B/Y-CDs and Ag+ [39]. The interaction between them was weakest under neutral conditions, so that the lowest point appeared in Fig. S6 at pH 7.0. The results showed that the difference between the fluorescence intensity ratios of B/Y-CDs and B/Y-CDs-Ag+ was the largest at pH 8.5 (Fig. S6). As a result, PBS buffer solution with a pH of 8.5 was chosen for the subsequent experiment. Additionally, the results demonstrated that the probe responded rapidly, with a maximum fluorescence ratio at a reaction time of 4 min, and the fluorescence ratio remained essentially unchanged as the reaction time was increased (Fig. S7). Therefore, 4 min was subsequently selected as the optimal reaction time.

The results of the interaction of the probe with Ag+ in PBS buffer at pH 8.5 were shown in Fig. 4 a, and the fluorescence intensity of the probe at 453 nm was gradually decreased with the increase of Ag+ concentration, while the fluorescence intensity at 584 nm was gradually increased. Fig. S8 showed the picture of color change in the solution of B/Y-CDs under 254 nm UV light after adding different concentrations of Ag+. The color of the solution was changed from green to yellow with increasing concentration of Ag+. The “Double-response-Reverse” type ratiometric fluorescent probe was constructed based on the double signal response mechanism of B/Y-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 4b–a good linear relationship existed between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe (F584/F453) and Ag+ concentration in the range of 1–90 μM with R2 of 0.9946. The limit of detection (LOD) for Ag+ was calculated as 364.7 nM using equation 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of the signals of the blank samples for 11 times). This method had a lower LOD and higher sensitivity compared to previously reported methods for detecting Ag+ (Table S1).

In order to assess the selectivity of this ratiometric fluorescent probe for Ag+, different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and Al3+) were added to the B/Y-CDs solution and their effects on the probe signal were measured. In Fig. 5 a, compared with the blank control group, with the addition of Ag+ the F584/F453 fluorescence intensity ratio was significantly increased. The addition of other metal ions to the B/Y-CDs solution did not have a significant effect on the change of the F584/F453 fluorescence intensity ratio, except for Fe3+ and Al3+. It has been reported that fluoride ion (F−) was commonly used as a masking agent for Fe3+ and Al3+ [40–42]. As illustrated in Fig. 5a&b, the addition of F− effectively eliminated the interference of Fe3+ and Al3+ on the fluorescence of B/Y-CDs. Fig. S9 showed the effect of the presence of Ag+ with each metal ions on the relative fluorescence intensity of B/Y-CDs. The F584/F453 fluorescence intensity ratio was little changed when Ag+ was present with each metal ions. In other words, the addition of other metal ions to the B/Y-CDs solution had little effect on the change of the F584/F453 fluorescence intensity ratio when Ag+ was also present with each metal ions. These findings suggested that the probe exhibited strong selectivity and could be utilized for the detection of Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for detection of Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction mechanism of Ag+ with B/Y-CDs was further investigated. Fig. 6 a illustrated that there was no overlap between the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Ag+ and the excitation as well as the emission spectra of B/Y-CDs, which implied that there was no occurrence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). In Fig. 6b, it was shown that after the addition of Ag+, the fluorescence lifetime of B/Y-CDs was reduced from 3.65 ns to 2.01 ns at 453 nm, indicating that dynamic quenching effect (DQE) occurred.

Prior reports showed that fluorescence enhancement of certain fluorophores could be induced by metal (such as Ag and Au) nanoparticles [43]. Therefore, it was hypothesized that the fluorescence enhancement at 584 nm might be due to the redox reaction of Ag+ with certain functional groups of B/Y-CDs. Ag+ was reduced to Ag0 and formed silver nanoparticles, and the interaction between the B/Y-CDs and the tiny silver nanoparticles formed might enhance the yellow fluorescence emission of the B/Y-CDs [44]. The feasibility of this hypothesis was examined by XPS spectroscopy after the reaction of Ag+ with B/Y-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6c, after the addition of Ag+, the full XPS spectrum showed peaks at 285.0 eV, 368.2 eV, 399.7 eV, and 532.1 eV, and the four peaks were attributed to C 1s, Ag 3d, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of Ag 3d (Fig. 6d) did not display a split peak and the peaks at 368.3 eV and 374.3 eV corresponded to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 of Ag0, respectively [45]. In comparison with the high-resolution spectrum of C1s of B/Y-CDs (Fig. 6f), the intensity of C–N/C–O peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s of B/Y-CDs after adding Ag+ was decreased (Fig. 6e). These findings indicated that certain nitrogen or oxygen-containing groups of B/Y-CDs underwent redox reactions with Ag+ as previous reports [45,46]. The results suggested that the interaction mechanism between Ag+ and B/Y-CDs involved DQE and redox reaction. These two mechanisms led to a decrease of fluorescence intensity at 453 nm and an increase of fluorescence intensity at 584 nm in the fluorescence spectra of B/Y-CDs, respectively (Fig. 7 ).

The feasibility of the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Ag+ in real samples was assessed using environmental water samples obtained from an industrial area. The water samples were spiked with varying concentrations of Ag+, and recovery experiments were conducted. The results, as shown in Table 1 , showed that the recoveries were 105.2 %, 95.5 % and 100.9 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 2 %, which demonstrated the feasibility and reliability of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on B/Y-CDs for the detection of Ag+ in real samples.

In brief, a straightforward one-pot solvothermal method was used to synthesize dual-emissive B/Y-CDs emitting at 453 nm and 584 nm, respectively. The ratiometric fluorescent probe constructed on the basis of the B/Y-CDs provided a simple and rapid method for the specific detection of Ag+ with a detection limit as low as 364.7 nM through the dynamic quenching and redox reaction between Ag+ and the B/Y-CDs. The feasibility of the method was verified using the probe detecting Ag+ in industrial water. This work provided a new strategy for the identification and detection of Ag+, which enriched the ways of detecting Ag+. Two different mechanisms of action were involved and thus two opposite signal changes were produced, B/Y-CDs had a more precise response to signaling and was expected to be valuable in environmental monitoring.

Mengying Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yuxiang Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yun Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (NSFC, Grant No. 22074004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.112143.
